name: Problem Decomposition
instructions: You will be presented with complex, real-world scenarios. For ordering questions, drag the steps into the correct logical sequence. For written questions, break the problem into clear, sequenced steps. Each question is timed — you will receive a warning before time runs out. You cannot go back to previous questions.

questions.prompt: A company is relocating its main office to a new building. Drag the following steps into the correct order.
questions.context: The move involves 200 employees, specialized equipment, active client projects that cannot be interrupted, and a lease that expires on a fixed date. Both buildings will be available for a 2-week overlap period.

items.text: Inventory all equipment, furniture, and materials that need to move
Set up workstations, network, and phone systems at the new location
Identify which teams and functions can move first with least disruption
Move the first wave of teams and verify they are fully operational
Communicate the move timeline and expectations to all employees
Move remaining teams in planned waves
Decommission the old office and return it per lease terms

questions.explanation: You must know what you have (inventory) before deciding who moves when (prioritize). Communicate the plan before executing. Infrastructure must be ready (set up new site) before anyone moves. First wave validates the process. Remaining waves follow. Decommission is last.

questions.prompt: A restaurant chain wants to add a new dish to its menu across all 30 locations. Drag the steps into the correct order.
questions.context: The dish requires a new ingredient from a supplier they haven't used before. It must meet nutritional labeling requirements and maintain consistency across locations. Kitchen staff will need training on preparation.

items.text: Source and vet the new supplier for quality, reliability, and cost
Develop and test the recipe in a test kitchen
Pilot the dish at 2–3 locations and gather customer feedback
Create nutritional labels and update menu materials
Train kitchen staff at all locations on preparation and plating
Negotiate supply agreements to cover all 30 locations
Launch the dish chain-wide and monitor quality and sales

questions.explanation: Recipe first (you need to know what you're making before sourcing). Source the key ingredient. Create nutritional info (regulatory requirement before serving to public). Pilot at a few locations to validate. Once validated, lock in supply at scale. Train all staff. Launch everywhere and monitor.

questions.prompt: A hospital needs to switch from paper-based patient intake forms to a digital system. Drag the steps into the correct order.
questions.context: The hospital sees 400+ patients per day. Staff range from tech-savvy to tech-resistant. Patient data is subject to strict privacy regulations. The paper system must remain functional as a fallback until the digital system is proven reliable.

items.text: Audit the current paper forms to identify all data fields and workflows
Define privacy and compliance requirements the digital system must meet
Select or build a digital system that meets the requirements
Run the digital system in parallel with paper forms in one department
Train staff on the new system with role-specific instructions
Migrate historical patient records into the digital system
Roll out to remaining departments based on pilot results
Retire the paper system once digital reliability is confirmed

questions.explanation: Audit current state first (know what exists). Define regulatory requirements (constraints before solution). Select/build the system. Migrate existing data (system needs data to be useful). Train staff (before they use it). Pilot in one department with parallel paper fallback. Expand to remaining departments. Retire paper only after proven reliability.

questions.prompt: An event planning team needs to organize a 500-person corporate conference. Drag the steps into the correct order.
questions.context: The conference is 6 months away. It includes keynote speakers, breakout sessions, catering, and live-streaming for remote attendees. The budget has already been approved.

items.text: Secure the venue and confirm the date
Define the conference agenda, themes, and session formats
Confirm keynote speakers and session presenters
Open registration and begin marketing
Arrange catering, A/V equipment, and live-streaming setup
Conduct a full run-through and technical rehearsal
Execute the event and manage day-of logistics

questions.explanation: Venue and date first (everything depends on location and timing). Define agenda (need content structure before recruiting speakers). Confirm speakers (need them before marketing). Open registration (need agenda and speakers to advertise). Arrange logistics (catering needs headcount estimates from registration). Rehearse. Execute.

questions.prompt: A regional bank is merging with another bank. The combined entity needs to unify customer accounts from both systems into a single system. Drag the steps into the correct order.
questions.context: Both banks have active customers who must experience no disruption. Account numbers, balances, and transaction histories must be preserved. Regulatory reporting must continue uninterrupted throughout the process.

items.text: Map data fields and account structures between the two systems
Choose which system will be the target and define the unified data model
Inventory all account types, products, and data formats in both systems
Run a pilot migration with a small batch of low-risk accounts
Build and test data transformation and migration tools
Migrate remaining accounts in planned batches with verification after each
Notify customers and provide guidance on any changes to their accounts
Decommission the retired system after a monitoring period

questions.explanation: Inventory both systems first (understand what exists). Choose the target system and unified model (strategic decision before mapping). Map fields between systems (translation layer). Build migration tools (automate the transformation). Pilot with low-risk accounts (validate before scale). Notify customers (before their accounts change). Migrate remaining accounts in batches with verification. Decommission retired system only after monitoring confirms success.

questions.prompt: Break this task into implementation steps (aim for 4–7 steps). Focus on the right sequence and key dependencies.
questions.context: A national retail chain wants to launch a customer loyalty program. Customers earn points on purchases and can redeem them for discounts. The program needs to work in all 150 physical stores and on the online store. Staff at each location need to be able to explain and enroll customers in the program.

constraints.placeholder_text: List your implementation steps in order. For each step, briefly note why it comes at that point in the sequence...

criteria: Identifies a design/definition phase (rules, earn rates, redemption mechanics)
Addresses the system/tooling needed to track points across channels (in-store + online)
Includes a staff training or enablement step before launch
Sequences a pilot or phased rollout before full-scale launch
Considers customer communication or marketing as a distinct step
Accounts for testing or validation before going live
Correct dependency ordering — does not place downstream steps before their prerequisites
Identifies information dependencies between steps — what early decisions (venue, program rules) constrain downstream choices (marketing, staffing, logistics)

levels.label: Exceptional
levels.description: Comprehensive, well-structured, identifies non-obvious issues/steps. Shows systems thinking.

levels.label: Strong
levels.description: Covers key points with good structure. May miss one subtle element but demonstrates clear reasoning.

levels.label: Adequate
levels.description: Identifies obvious issues/steps but lacks depth or misses important considerations.

levels.label: Weak
levels.description: Superficial or partially correct. Shows some reasoning but significant gaps.

levels.label: Poor
levels.description: Misunderstands the task, provides irrelevant response, or demonstrates no structured thinking.

levels.label: No Response
levels.description: Blank or completely off-topic.

questions.sample_strong_response:
Step 1: Define the program structure. Before building anything, define the rules: how do customers earn points (per dollar? per visit?), what's the redemption model (discounts? free items? tiers?), are there expiration rules? This comes first because every downstream decision — system requirements, staff training, marketing — depends on these rules.

Step 2: Select or build the loyalty platform. With the rules defined, evaluate systems that can track points across 150 physical stores AND the online store in real time. The cross-channel requirement is the critical constraint — the system must handle a customer earning points in-store and redeeming online, or vice versa. Integration with existing POS systems and the e-commerce platform is the key technical decision.

Step 3: Configure and test the system. Set up the platform with the program rules from Step 1, integrate with POS and online checkout, and test end-to-end: earn in-store, redeem online, and the reverse. Validate that point balances sync correctly across channels. This must be complete before any customer or staff sees the program.

Step 4: Train store staff. Staff at 150 locations need to explain the program, enroll customers, and troubleshoot basic issues (points not appearing, redemption failures). Training before launch is essential — a confused staff member will create a negative first impression that undermines enrollment. Consider a train-the-trainer model given the scale.

Step 5: Pilot at 5–10 stores. Run the program at a small subset of locations for 2–4 weeks before full rollout. This catches configuration issues, staff readiness gaps, and customer experience problems at a manageable scale. The pilot also produces real enrollment and redemption data to validate the program economics.

Step 6: Full launch with marketing. Roll out to all 150 stores and online, supported by customer-facing marketing (in-store signage, email campaigns, website promotion). Marketing comes after the system works and staff are trained — launching marketing before the program is ready creates demand you can't fulfill.

questions.prompt: Break this task into implementation steps (aim for 4–7 steps). Focus on the right sequence and key dependencies.
questions.context: A mid-size company currently tracks employee time-off requests via email and spreadsheets. They want to replace this with a structured process where employees submit requests, managers approve or deny them, and remaining balances update automatically. The company has 300 employees across 4 offices in different time zones.

constraints.placeholder_text: List your implementation steps in order. For each step, briefly note why it comes at that point in the sequence...

criteria: Identifies a requirements/discovery phase (current policies, leave types, approval chains)
Addresses tool selection or system design as a distinct step
Accounts for migrating existing balance/history data into the new system
Includes training or change management for employees and managers
Sequences a pilot or phased rollout before company-wide deployment
Considers timezone or cross-office implications
Correct dependency ordering — discovery before design, design before build, training before launch
Considers what happens if a step fails or is delayed — contingency or dependency-aware ordering

levels.label: Exceptional
levels.description: Comprehensive, well-structured, identifies non-obvious issues/steps. Shows systems thinking.

levels.label: Strong
levels.description: Covers key points with good structure. May miss one subtle element but demonstrates clear reasoning.

levels.label: Adequate
levels.description: Identifies obvious issues/steps but lacks depth or misses important considerations.

levels.label: Weak
levels.description: Superficial or partially correct. Shows some reasoning but significant gaps.

levels.label: Poor
levels.description: Misunderstands the task, provides irrelevant response, or demonstrates no structured thinking.

levels.label: No Response
levels.description: Blank or completely off-topic.

questions.sample_strong_response:
Step 1: Discovery and requirements gathering. Before selecting any tool, document the current state: what leave types exist (PTO, sick, personal, etc.), who approves what (direct manager? skip-level? HR for extended leave?), how are balances calculated (accrual vs. annual grant?), and are there policies that vary by office or timezone? This step comes first because every downstream decision depends on understanding the current rules. The timezone dimension is critical: a request submitted at 5pm in one office might land on a manager's desk at 2pm in another.

Step 2: Tool evaluation and selection. With requirements documented, evaluate options against them. Key criteria: does it support the approval chains from Step 1? Can it handle timezone-aware workflows? Does it integrate with existing payroll or HR systems? Can it import historical data?

Step 3: System configuration and data migration. Configure the tool with the policies and approval chains from Steps 1–2. Migrate existing leave balances from spreadsheets. Critically, validate migrated data against source spreadsheets for a sample of employees — migration errors here will erode trust immediately.

Step 4: Training and change management. Train both managers (how to approve/deny, handle edge cases) and employees (how to submit, check balances). Change management matters because if submitting a request is harder than sending an email, adoption will be low.

Step 5: Pilot with one office. Run the system with a single office for 2–4 weeks. This catches configuration issues and workflow gaps before affecting all 300 employees. Ideally the pilot office interacts with managers in other timezones to test cross-office workflows.

Step 6: Full rollout and decommission. Roll out to remaining offices in waves. Once all are live and stable, formally shut down the email/spreadsheet process — keeping both running creates confusion about the source of truth.

questions.prompt: Break this task into implementation steps (aim for 4–7 steps). Focus on the right sequence and key dependencies.
questions.context: A logistics company has 3 regional warehouses that each manage inventory independently using their own tracking methods. They want to unify inventory visibility so that any warehouse can see real-time stock levels at the other two. Orders should be fulfillable from whichever warehouse has stock, rather than only from the warehouse closest to the customer. The warehouses operate on different schedules and use different labeling conventions.

constraints.placeholder_text: List your implementation steps in order. For each step, briefly note why it comes at that point in the sequence...

criteria: Identifies an audit/discovery step to understand each warehouse's current tracking method and labeling system
Addresses standardization of product labeling or SKU conventions across warehouses
Includes selection or design of a unified inventory visibility system
Considers data migration or synchronization of current inventory into the unified system
Addresses the order routing or fulfillment logic change (which warehouse ships?)
Includes testing or pilot phase before full rollout
Accounts for operational differences (schedules, processes) across warehouses
Sequences dependencies correctly — standardize before unifying, unify before rerouting fulfillment
Identifies that data migration requires a validation/reconciliation step, not just a transfer step

levels.label: Exceptional
levels.description: Comprehensive, well-structured, identifies non-obvious issues/steps. Shows systems thinking.

levels.label: Strong
levels.description: Covers key points with good structure. May miss one subtle element but demonstrates clear reasoning.

levels.label: Adequate
levels.description: Identifies obvious issues/steps but lacks depth or misses important considerations.

levels.label: Weak
levels.description: Superficial or partially correct. Shows some reasoning but significant gaps.

levels.label: Poor
levels.description: Misunderstands the task, provides irrelevant response, or demonstrates no structured thinking.

levels.label: No Response
levels.description: Blank or completely off-topic.

questions.sample_strong_response:
Step 1: Inventory and assessment. Before unifying anything, document what each warehouse currently tracks: what fields exist, what units are used (each vs. case vs. pallet?), how are items identified (SKU? barcode? internal code?), and what's the data quality like? If one warehouse has clean digital records and another has handwritten logs, the migration complexity is very different. This comes first because you can't design a unified system without knowing what you're unifying.

Step 2: Define the unified data model. Based on the inventory, decide: one item ID scheme (which one wins, or do you create a new one?), standard units of measure, required fields for every item, and how to handle items that exist in one warehouse's system but not another's. This is a design decision, not a technical one — it requires input from operations staff who understand why each warehouse does things differently.

Step 3: Build data transformation and mapping. For each warehouse, create the rules to convert their current data format into the unified model. This includes mapping old IDs to new IDs, converting units, and handling discrepancies (what if Warehouse A says there are 500 units and Warehouse B's records for the same item say 450?). Discrepancy resolution rules must be defined before migration, not discovered during it.

Step 4: Migrate and reconcile. Run the migration for one warehouse first, then validate: does the unified system's inventory match a physical count sample? Do the numbers reconcile with the source records? Fix discrepancies before migrating the next warehouse. Reconciliation is not optional — it's the step that determines whether the unified system is trustworthy.

Step 5: Train warehouse staff and update processes. Staff need to use the unified system for receiving, picking, and cycle counts. If the ID scheme or units changed, labels and procedures need updating. Training before full cutover prevents errors that would immediately undermine confidence in the new system.

Step 6: Cutover and monitor. Switch all three warehouses to the unified system. Run parallel tracking for a defined period (e.g., 2 weeks) to catch any discrepancies the migration missed. Decommission the old systems only after the monitoring period confirms accuracy.

questions.prompt: Break this task into implementation steps (aim for 4–7 steps). Focus on the right sequence and key dependencies.
questions.context: A school district with 20 schools currently handles student enrollment on paper. Each school maintains its own waitlists and manually tracks which students have been accepted, declined, or are pending. The district wants a centralized enrollment process where parents apply once and are matched to available spots across all schools based on preferences, proximity, and sibling priority. The transition must happen before the next enrollment cycle in 5 months.

constraints.placeholder_text: List your implementation steps in order. For each step, briefly note why it comes at that point in the sequence...

criteria: Identifies need to define the matching/priority rules before building anything
Addresses data gathering — current enrollment, capacity, waitlists from each school
Includes system design or selection to handle centralized applications and matching
Accounts for parent/family communication and enrollment instructions
Includes training for school staff who will use or support the new process
Considers a testing or simulation phase (dry run of the matching algorithm/logic)
Recognizes the hard deadline as a constraint that shapes the plan
Correct dependency ordering — rules before system, system before testing, communication before enrollment opens
Considers the information handoff between discovery and design — what gets lost if you skip it

levels.label: Exceptional
levels.description: Comprehensive, well-structured, identifies non-obvious issues/steps. Shows systems thinking.

levels.label: Strong
levels.description: Covers key points with good structure. May miss one subtle element but demonstrates clear reasoning.

levels.label: Adequate
levels.description: Identifies obvious issues/steps but lacks depth or misses important considerations.

levels.label: Weak
levels.description: Superficial or partially correct. Shows some reasoning but significant gaps.

levels.label: Poor
levels.description: Misunderstands the task, provides irrelevant response, or demonstrates no structured thinking.

levels.label: No Response
levels.description: Blank or completely off-topic.

questions.sample_strong_response:
Step 1: Discovery across all 20 schools. Before designing anything, understand the current state: what enrollment data does each school collect? What systems do they use (paper, spreadsheets, different software)? What are the data formats and quality levels? Are there schools with unique requirements (special programs, language-specific forms, capacity constraints)? The 5-month deadline makes this urgent — discovery that drags on compresses everything downstream.

Step 2: Define the centralized system requirements. Based on discovery, define what the unified system needs: required fields, data validation rules, access permissions (who at each school can enter/edit data?), and reporting requirements for the district. The design must accommodate the 20 schools' actual variety, not an idealized version of it. If discovery found that 5 schools track data that others don't, the design must decide whether to require it from all or make it optional.

Step 3: Select or build the system. Given the 5-month deadline, buying is likely faster than building. Evaluate options against the requirements from Step 2. Integration with existing district systems (student information system, transportation, lunch program) is probably a hard constraint.

Step 4: Data migration and validation. Migrate existing enrollment data from all 20 schools into the new system. This is the highest-risk step: 20 different source formats, varying data quality, and the possibility of duplicate records across schools. Build validation checks that flag anomalies (students enrolled in two schools, missing required fields, impossible dates). Validate a sample with school registrars before declaring migration complete.

Step 5: Train school staff. Each school's registrar or enrollment coordinator needs to use the system. Given 20 schools, consider training in cohorts grouped by current technical readiness. Provide reference materials, not just live training — staff will need to look things up during the first enrollment cycle.

Step 6: Pilot during a low-volume period. If possible, pilot with 3–5 schools before the deadline. If the deadline doesn't allow sequential rollout, at minimum do a dry run: have each school enter a set of test records and verify the data flows correctly to the district level.

Step 7: Full launch with support. Go live across all 20 schools with dedicated support available for the first enrollment cycle. Monitor data quality centrally and flag issues early. The first cycle will surface edge cases that no amount of planning can predict — having a response mechanism ready is the difference between a bumpy launch and a failed one.

questions.prompt: A manufacturing company wants to switch from a single supplier to a dual-supplier model for a critical raw material. Drag the steps into the correct order.
questions.context: The current supplier provides 100% of the material. The company cannot risk any supply gap during the transition. Quality standards are strict — the new supplier's material must pass the same specifications. Existing contracts have 90-day termination notice requirements.

items.text: Identify and evaluate potential second suppliers
Request and test samples from the top candidate suppliers
Negotiate terms and contracts with the chosen second supplier
Run a small production batch using the new supplier's material
Renegotiate the existing supplier's contract to reflect reduced volume
Gradually shift a percentage of orders to the new supplier
Monitor quality and delivery performance from both suppliers

questions.explanation: Identify candidates first. Test samples before committing. Run a production trial to validate real-world performance. Only then negotiate contracts (you have leverage and confidence). Renegotiate existing contract (adjust volume). Gradually shift orders (don't flip overnight). Monitor both suppliers ongoing.
